problems.title: Any Set is Compact in Co-finite Topology
problems.problem: Suppose $X$ is an infinite set equipped with the cofinite topology. Prove that $X$ is compact.
problems.hint: • The complement of any non-empty open set has only finitely many elements. Now use the definition of compactness to show that the space is compact.
problems.solution: Let $\mathcal{C} \coloneqq \{ U_\alpha \}_{\alpha \in I} $ be an open cover of $X$. Since for each $\alpha \in I$ the set $U_\alpha $ is open and hence $U_{\alpha } = \varnothing $ or $X \setminus U_\alpha $ is a finite set. Let $x_0 \in X$ and let $x_0 \in U_{\alpha _0}$ for some $\alpha _0 \in I$. This implies $X \setminus U_{\alpha _0}$ is finite, that is, \[ X \setminus U_{\alpha _0} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k\}. \] Choose $\alpha _i \in I$ such that $x_i \in U_{\alpha _i}$. Thus, we have a finite subcover of $\mathcal{C} $, namely, $\{ U_{\alpha _0}, U_{\alpha _1}, \dots, U_{\alpha _k} \} $. Therefore, $X$ is compact.

problems.title: Some Problem
problems.problem:
On the real line $\mathbb{R} $, consider the collection of subsets 
    \[
        \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow } \coloneqq \{ \varnothing , \mathbb{R}  \}  \cup \{ (-\infty ,a): a \in \mathbb{R}  \}  .
    \] 
    Show that $\mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$ is a topology on $\mathbb{R} $.
problems.hint:
• If $\{ U_\alpha = (-\infty ,\alpha ) : \alpha \in I \}\subset \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$, then consider the supremum of all such $\alpha $.
  • If $F_i = (-\infty , \alpha _i) \in \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$, $i =1,2,\dots,n $, then consider the minimum of such $\alpha _i$.
problems.solution:
Recall that $\mathcal{T} $ is a topology on a set $X$, if the following holds:
  1. $X, \varnothing \in \mathcal{T} $;
  2. If $U_\alpha \in \mathcal{T} $ for $\alpha \in I$, then $ \cup U_\alpha \in \mathcal{T} $;
  3. If $F_i \in \mathcal{T} $ for $i = 1,2,\dots,n$, then $ \cap F_i \in \mathcal{T} $.
We will verify each of these conditions.
  1. True as it is given that $\varnothing , \mathbb{R} \in \mathcal{T}_{\leftarrow }.$
  2. Let $\{ U_\alpha = (-\infty ,\alpha ) : \alpha \in I \}\subset \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$, where $I$ is an index set. We need to show that $ \cup (-\infty ,\alpha ) \in \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$. Consider the collection $\mathcal{A} = \{\alpha \in \mathbb{R} : \alpha \in I\}$, and let $\alpha _0 = \sup \mathcal{A} $. It is clear that $\alpha _0 \in \mathbb{R} $ and also $ \cup U_\alpha = (-\infty ,\alpha _0)\in \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$.
  3. Let $F_i = (-\infty , \alpha _i) \in \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$, $i =1,2,\dots,n $. Since the collection $\{ \alpha _i \in \mathbb{R} : i = 1,2,\dots, n \} $ is finite, let $\alpha _0$ is the minimum of the collection and $ \cap (-\infty ,\alpha _i) = (-\infty ,\alpha _0) \in \mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$.
Thus, $\mathcal{T} _{\leftarrow }$ is a topology on $\mathbb{R} $.

problems.title: Fixed point set of a continuous function is closed
problems.problem:
Let $\mathbb{R} $ be equipped with the usual Euclidean topology. If $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $ is a continuous function, show that the set of points which are left fixed by $f$ is a closed subset of $\mathbb{R} $.
Also generalize the same for a Hausdorff topological space $X$ and a continuous function $f: X \to X$.
problems.hint:
• Consider $g(x) = f(x) - x$.
  • Use the condition that the topology is Hausdorff. Show that the complement is open.
problems.solution:
Let \[ \mathcal{F} = \{ x\in \mathbb{R} : f(x) = x \} . \] We need to show that $\mathcal{F} $ is closed subset of $\mathbb{R} .$ Consider a function $g: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $ \[ g(x) = f(x) - x. \] It is clear that $g$ is continuous and $\mathcal{F} = g^{-1} (\{ 0 \} )$. Since $\{ 0 \} $ is a closed set and $g$ is continuous, $\mathcal{F} = g^{-1} \{ 0 \} $ is closed in $\mathbb{R} $.
This problem can be solved for an arbitrary Hausdorff topological space $X$ and a continuous function on $X$. More precisely, if $X$ is a topological space which is Hausdorff and $f: X \to X$ is continuous, then the fixed point set of $f$ is closed in $X$ . We will show that $\mathcal{F} ^c = \mathbb{R} \setminus \mathcal{F} $ is open in $\mathbb{R} $. Let $a \in \mathcal{F} ^c$, which implies $f(a) \neq a$. As the standard topology on $\mathbb{R} $ is Hausdorff, we can find neighborhoods $U \text{ and } V$ of $f(a) \text{ and } a$, respectively, such that $U \cap V = \varnothing .$ Since $f$ is continuous, $f^{-1} (V)$ is an open neighborhood of $a$ and hence $U \cap f^{-1} (V)$ is an open neighborhood of $a$. We now claim that $\mathcal{O} \coloneqq U \cap f^{-1} (V) \subseteq \mathcal{F} ^c$.
To the contrary, if $x \in \mathcal{O} \cap \mathcal{F} $, then $x \in U, x \in f^{-1} (V)$ and $x \in \mathcal{F} $ which implies $f(x) = x$. Since \[ x \in f^{-1} (V) \implies f(x) \in V \implies x = f(x) \in V \implies x \in U \cap V, \] a contradiction.

problems.title: A basis for R^2
problems.problem: Let $\mathcal{B} $ be the collection of all open discs with all possible radii and center at any point. Show that $\mathcal{B} $ is a basis for the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$.
problems.hint: • Draw pictures to show that if $x \in B_1 \cap B_2$ for some $B_1, B_2 \in \mathcal{B}$, then there exists $B_3 \in \mathcal{B}$ such that \[ x \in B_3 \subseteq B_1 \cap B_2. \]
problems.solution:
Recall that the definition of a basis for a topology on $X$. A collection $\mathcal{B}$ of subsets of $X$ is called a basis for a topology on $X$ if it satisfies the following two conditions:
  1. Covering condition: For every $x \in X$, there exists $B \in \mathcal{B}$ such that $x \in B$.
  2. Intersection condition: If $x \in B_1 \cap B_2$ for some $B_1, B_2 \in \mathcal{B}$, then there exists $B_3 \in \mathcal{B}$ such that \[ x \in B_3 \subseteq B_1 \cap B_2. \]
Note that \[ \mathcal{B} = \left\{ D(\mathbf{x} ,r): \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R} ^2, \text{ and } r > 0 \right\}, \] where \[ D(\mathbf{x} ,r) = \left\{ \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R} ^2 : \lVert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \rVert < r \right\} . \]
  1. Let $\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R} ^2$. Then $\mathbf{a} \in D(\mathbf{a} , 1)$ and hence the covering condition satisfies.
  2. Let $B_1 = D(\mathbf{x} _1,r_1)$ and $B_2 = D(\mathbf{x} _2,r_2)$ be elements of $\mathcal{B}$, and suppose that \[ \mathbf{x} \in B_1 \cap B_2. \] Then \[ \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x} _1\| < r_1 \quad \text{and} \quad \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x} _2\| < r_2. \] Define \[ \varepsilon = \min \{ r_1 - \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x} _1\|,\; r_2 - \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x} _2\| \} > 0. \] In the below picture, $\varepsilon = \min \{ r,s \} $.
Consider the open disc $B_3 = D(x,\varepsilon)$. For any $y \in B_3$, we have \begin{align*} \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x} _1\| \le \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x} \| + \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x} _1\| < \varepsilon + \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x} _1\| \le r_1, \end{align*} so $\mathbf{y} \in B_1$. Similarly, $\mathbf{y} \in B_2$. Thus, \[ \mathbf{x} \in B_3 \subseteq B_1 \cap B_2, \] Since $\mathcal{B}$ satisfies both basis axioms, it is a basis for a topology on $\mathbb{R}^2$.

problems.title: NBHM PhD 2005 - Distance between x-axis and the graph y = 1/x
problems.hint: • $d(A,B) = \inf \{ d(a,b): a \in A \text{ and } B \in B\}$.
problems.solution:
Given that \begin{align*} A & = \{ (x,y) \in \mathbb{R} ^2: y = 0 \} \text{ and } \\ B & = \{ (x,y) \in \mathbb{R} ^2: xy = 1 \}. \end{align*} We need to find the distance between $A$ and $B$. Observe that for a fix $ y = \left( b, \frac{1}{b} \right) \in B$ \begin{align*} d(A,b) & = \inf \{ d(x,y): x \in A\} \\ & = d\left( (b,0), \left( b, \frac{1}{b} \right) \right) \\ & = \frac{1}{\vert b \vert }. \end{align*}
Thus, \begin{align*} d(A, B) & = \inf \{ d(x,y): x \in A \text{ and } y \in B\} \\ & = \inf \left\{ \frac{1}{\vert b \vert } : b \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \{ 0 \} \right\} \\ & = 0. \end{align*}

problems.title: A discontinuous function is continuous in lower limit topology
problems.problem:
Let $\mathcal{T} _s $ be the usual topology on the real line $\mathbb{R} $ and $\mathcal{T} _u$ be the upper limit topology on $\mathbb{R} $ which is generated by the open-closed intervals $(a, b]$. Furthermore, let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $ be defined by \[ f(x) = \begin{cases} x, &\text{ if } x \leq 1 ;\\ x + 2, &\text{ if } x > 1. \end{cases} \]
  1. Show that $f$ is not continuous if both side we have $\mathcal{T} _s$ topology.
  2. Show that $f$ is continuous if both side we have $\mathcal{T} _u$ topology.
problems.hint:
• For the standard topology look at the graph of the function and $U = (-3,2)$ and see what is the inverse image.
  • For the upper limit topology, show that the preimage of basis is open, that is, $f^{-1} ((a,b])$ is open.
problems.solution:
The given function is \[ f(x) = \begin{cases} x, &\text{ if } x \leq 1 ;\\ x + 2, &\text{ if } x > 1. \end{cases} \] We will show that this function is not continuous if we have the standard topology and continuous if we put the upper limit topology. Just to recall that a function is continuous if it pulls back an open set to an open set. Since $f^{-1} \left( \cup U_{\alpha } \right) = \cup f^{-1} (U_\alpha )$, so it is enough to check for basis.
  1. Look at the figure below. Take $U = (-3,2) \in \mathcal{T} _s$. Then \begin{align*} f^{-1} (U) & = f^{-1} \left( (-3,1) \cup (1,2) \right) \\ & = f^{-1} (-3, 1] \cup f^{-1} (1,2) \\ & = (-3,1] \cup \varnothing = (-3,1] \notin \mathcal{T} _s. \end{align*} Hence, $f$ is not continuous with the standard topology.
  2. Now to show that $f$ is continuous with the lower limit topology, we will show that $f^{-1} ((a,b])$ is open for any $a,b \in \mathbb{R} $. Note that \[ f^{-1} ((a,b]) = \begin{cases} (a,b], &\text{ if } a < b \leq 1 ;\\ (a,1], &\text{ if } a < b \leq 3 ;\\ (a,b-2], &\text{ if } a < 1 < 3 < b ;\\ \varnothing, &\text{ if } 1\leq a < b \leq 3;\\ (1, b-2], &\text{ if } 1\leq a < 3 < b;\\ (a-2, b-2], &\text{ if } 3 \leq a < b. \end{cases} \] Thus, in either case $f^{-1} ((a,b]) in \mathcal{T} _u$ and hence $f $ is continuous.

problems.title: Contractible spaces are path connected
problems.problem: Show that every contractible space is path connected.
problems.hint: • Contractible space $X$ means the identity function is nullhomotopic. Use the homotopy that you will get by nullhomotopic condition.
problems.solution:
Let $X$ be a contractible topological space. Recall that $X$ is contractible if the identity function $\mathrm{id}_X : X \to X$ is nullhomotopic, that is there exists $x_0 \in X$ such that $\mathrm{id}_X \simeq c_{x_0}$, where $c_{x_0}: X \to X$ is the constant function which takes the value $x_0$ for any $x \in X$.
Let $a,b \in X$. We need to show that there exists a path in $X$ joining $a$ to $b$. We will give a path joining $a$ to $x_0$ and then $x_0$ to $b$. Since $X$ is contractible, the identity function $\mathrm{id}_X : X \to X $ is homotopic to $c_{x_0}$. That is, there exists a homotopy \begin{gather*} H: X \times [0,1] \to X, \\ H(x,0) = x \text{ and } H(x,1)= x_0,\ \forall\ x \in X. \end{gather*} Define a path \[ \gamma : [0,1] \to X, \quad \gamma (t) = H(a, t). \] It is clear that $\gamma $ is continuous as $H$ is continuous. Note that \[ \gamma (0) = H(a,0) = a \text{ and } \gamma (1) = H(a, 1) = x_0. \] Thus, we get a path from $a$ to $x_0$.
In a similar way, the path \[ \eta (t) = H(b,1-t), \quad t \in [0,1] \] joins $x_0$ to $b$. Now concatenating these two paths we get a path joining $a$ to $b$. More precisely, \[ \alpha (t) = \begin{cases} \gamma (2t), &\text{ if } 0 \leq t \leq \frac{1}{2} ;\\ \eta(1-2t) , &\text{ if } \frac{1}{2} \leq t \leq 1 \end{cases} \] is a path from $a$ to $b$. The function $\alpha $ is continuous because of the pasting lemma.

problems.title: Union of connected set is connected
problems.problem:
Let $X$ be a topological space and let $\{A_i\}_{i \in I}$ be any family of connected subsets of $X$ such that
    \[
        \bigcap_{i \in I} A_i \neq \emptyset.
    \]
    Prove that the union $\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i$ is connected.
problems.hint:
• Suppose $\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i = U \cup V$ where $U$ and $V$ are disjoint, nonempty, relatively open sets in the subspace topology.
  • Pick any point $x \in \bigcap_{i \in I} A_i$. Without loss of generality, assume $x \in U$.
  • Show that every $A_i$ must lie entirely in $U$ (using the connectedness of each $A_i$).
  • Conclude that $V = \emptyset$, yielding a contradiction.
problems.solution:
Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that $\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i$ is disconnected. Then there exist nonempty sets $U$ and $V$, open in the subspace topology on $\bigcup A_i$, such that \[ \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i = (U \cap \cup A_i) \cup (V \cap \cup A_i), \quad (U \cap \cup A_i) \cap (V \cap \cup A_i) = \emptyset, \] and both $U \cap \bigcup A_i$ and $V \cap \bigcup A_i$ are nonempty. Let $x \in \bigcap_{i \in I} A_i$ (nonempty by hypothesis). Then $x \in \bigcup A_i$, so $x$ belongs to exactly one of the two pieces; say $x \in U \cap \bigcup A_i$.
Now fix an arbitrary $j \in I$. Consider $A_j$. We know $x \in A_j$ and $A_j$ is connected. The sets $A_j \cap U$ and $A_j \cap V$ are relatively open in $A_j$, disjoint, and their union is $A_j$. By connectedness of $A_j$, one of them must be empty. Since $x \in A_j \cap U$, it follows that $A_j \cap V = \emptyset$, so $A_j \subseteq U$. As this holds for every $j$, we obtain \[ \bigcup_{i \in I} A_i \subseteq U \implies V \cap \bigcup A_i = \emptyset, \] contradicting the assumption that $V \cap \bigcup A_i \neq \emptyset$. Therefore $\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i$ is connected.

problems.title: f is a closed map
problems.problem: Let $X$ be a compact topological space and let $f \colon X \to Y$ be a continuous map, where $Y$ is a Hausdorff space. Prove that $f$ is a closed map (i.e., the image of every closed set in $X$ is closed in $Y$).
problems.hint:
• A continuous function maps a compact set to a compact set.
  • Inside a Hausdorff space any compact set is closed.
problems.solution: Let $C \subseteq X$ be closed. Since $X$ is compact, $C$ is compact. We will show that $f(C)$ is closed in $Y$. Since $f$ is continuous and $C$ is compact, implies $f(C)$ is also compact. Since $Y$ is Hausdorff, $f(C)$ is closed in $Y$. This proves that $f$ is a closed map.

problems.title: A is closed if and only if boundary of A subset of A
problems.problem: Prove that a set $A$ is closed in $X$ if and only if $\partial A \subset A$, where $\partial A$ is the topological boundary of $A$.
problems.hint:
• For any set $A$, the boundary is $\partial A = \mathrm{Cl}(A) - \mathrm{int}(A) $.
  • A set $A$ is closed if and only if $\mathrm{Cl} (A) = A$.
problems.solution:
Recall that for the set $A$, \[ \partial A = \mathrm{Cl}(A) - \mathrm{int}(A), \] where $\mathrm{Cl}(A) $ is the closure of $A$ and $\mathrm{int}(A) $ is the interior of $A$.
Let $A$ be a closed set in $X$. Thus, \begin{align*} \mathrm{Cl}(A) = A & \implies \mathrm{Cl}(A) - \mathrm{int}(A) = A - \mathrm{int}(A) \\ & \implies \partial A = A - \mathrm{int}(A) \subset A. \end{align*}
On the other hand, let $\partial A \subset \mathrm{Cl}(A) - \mathrm{int}(A) $. We will prove that $A$ is closed in $X$, that is, $\mathrm{Cl}(A) = A $. We already have $A \subseteq \mathrm{Cl} (A)$. For $\mathrm{Cl}(A) \subseteq A $, let $x \in \mathrm{Cl} (A)$. Note that we have $\mathrm{int}(A) \subset A$. If $x \in \mathrm{int}(A) $, then we are done. So, let $x \in \mathrm{Cl} (A) - \mathrm{int}(A)$. Since, \begin{align*} x \in \mathrm{Cl} (A) - \mathrm{int}(A) = \partial A \subset A \implies \mathrm{Cl} (A) \subseteq A. \end{align*} Thus, $A = \mathrm{Cl} (A)$.

problems.title: Closed balls are closed sets
problems.problem: In a metric space, show that a closed ball is closed.
problems.hint: • Show that the complement is open. For that draw picture and use triangle inequality.
problems.solution:
Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. Let $x_0 \in X$ and \[ B = B[x_0; r] := \left\{ x \in X : d(x, x_0) < r \right\} \] be the closed ball of radius $r$ centred at $x_0$. We need to show that $B$ is closed. We will show that the complement is open. Let $y \in X - B$. Let $r_0 = d(x_0,y) - r$ which implies $d(x_0,y) > r$. We claim that $B(y, r_0) \subseteq X\setminus B$. For that, let $z \in B(y, r_0)$ which implies $d(z,y) < r_0$.
Consider, \begin{align*} d(x_0,y) \leq d(x_0,z) + d(z,y) & \implies d(x_0,z) \geq d(x_0,y) - d(z,y) \\ & \implies d(z_0,z) > r_0 + r - r_0 = r. \end{align*} Thus, $B(y,r_0) \subseteq X\setminus B$ and hence $B$ is a closed set.

problems.title: Intersection of two open balls contains an open ball
problems.problem:
Let $\mathbf{x} ,\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R} ^n$, and $r = \lVert \mathbf{x} -\mathbf{y}  \rVert $. Show that
    \[
        B\left( \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y} }{2}, \frac{r}{2} \right) \subset B(\mathbf{x} ,r)  \cap B(\mathbf{y} ,r),
    \]
    where $B(\mathbf{x} ,r) = \{\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R} ^n: \lVert \mathbf{x} -\mathbf{z}  \rVert < r \}$.
problems.hint:
• Note that $\lVert \mathbf{x} -\mathbf{y} \rVert = r$ implies $\left\lVert \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}}{2} - \mathbf{x} \right\rVert = \left\lVert \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}}{2} - \mathbf{y} \right\rVert = \frac{r}{2}$.
  • Use the triangle inequality to show that if $\mathbf{z} \in B\left( \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y} }{2}, \frac{r}{2} \right)$, then $\mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{x}, r)$ and $\mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{y}, r)$.
problems.solution:
Let $\mathbf{z} \in B\left( \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y} }{2}, \frac{r}{2} \right)$. Then, \[ \left\lVert \mathbf{z} - \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}}{2} \right\rVert < \frac{r}{2}. \] We need to show that $\mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{x}, r) \cap B(\mathbf{y}, r)$.
First, we show $\mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{x}, r)$. By the triangle inequality, \[ \lVert \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{x} \rVert \leq \left\lVert \mathbf{z} - \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}}{2} \right\rVert + \left\lVert \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}}{2} - \mathbf{x} \right\rVert. \] Since $\left\lVert \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}}{2} - \mathbf{x} \right\rVert = \frac{1}{2}\lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x} \rVert = \frac{r}{2}$, we have \[ \lVert \mathbf{z} - \mathbf{x} \rVert < \frac{r}{2} + \frac{r}{2} = r. \] Thus, $\mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{x}, r)$.
Similarly, we can show that $\mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{y}, r)$. Therefore, \[ \mathbf{z} \in B(\mathbf{x}, r) \cap B(\mathbf{y}, r) \] and hence \[ B\left( \frac{\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y} }{2}, \frac{r}{2} \right) \subset B(\mathbf{x} ,r) \cap B(\mathbf{y} ,r). \]

problems.title: Half open rectangles generate a finer topology than the usual topology
problems.problem:
Let $\mathcal{T} $ be the topology on the plane $\mathbb{R} ^2$ generated by the half open rectangles of the form,
    \[
        [a,b) \times [c,d) = \{ (x,y) : a \leq x < b, c \leq y < d \} 
    \]
    where $a,b,c,d \in \mathbb{R} $. Show that $\mathcal{T} $ is strictly finer than the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$.
problems.hint:
• Show that $\mathcal{T} $ is finer than the usual topology by showing that every open set in the usual topology is also open in $\mathcal{T} $.
  • For that draw a picture and show that for every point in an open set in the usual topology, we can find a half open rectangle that contains that point and is contained in the open set.
problems.solution:
Let $\mathcal{T} $ be the topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$ generated by the half open rectangles of the form, \[ [a,b) \times [c,d) = \{ (x,y) : a \leq x < b, c \leq y < d \} \] where $a,b,c,d \in \mathbb{R} $. We will show that $\mathcal{T} $ is strictly finer than the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$.
First, we show that $\mathcal{T} $ is finer than the usual topology. Let $U$ be an open set in the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$. We need to show that $U$ is also open in $\mathcal{T} $. Let $(x,y) \in U$. Since $U$ is open in the usual topology, there exists an open ball $B((x,y), r)$ such that $B((x,y), r) \subseteq U$. Consider the half open rectangle \[ R = \left[ x-\frac{r}{2}, x+\frac{r}{2} \right) \times \left[ y-\frac{r}{2}, y+\frac{r}{2} \right). \] It is clear that $R$ is an element of $\mathcal{T} $. Since any point in $R$ is at a distance is at distance at most $\frac{r}{2}$ from $(x,y)$, we have $R \subseteq B((x,y), r)$. Thus, we have \[ R = \left[ x-\frac{r}{2}, x+\frac{r}{2} \right) \times \left[ y-\frac{r}{2}, y+\frac{r}{2} \right) \subseteq B((x,y), r) \subseteq U. \]
Thus, for every point $(x,y) \in U$, we can find a half open rectangle $R$ such that $(x,y) \in R \subseteq U$. This shows that $U$ is open in $\mathcal{T} $. Hence, $\mathcal{T} $ is finer than the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$.
Now we show that $\mathcal{T} $ is strictly finer than the usual topology. Consider the half open rectangle \[ R = [0,1) \times [0,1). \] It is clear that $R$ is an element of $\mathcal{T} $. We will show that $R$ is not open in the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$. Let $(0,0) \in R$. If $R$ is open in the usual topology, then there exists an open ball $B((0,0), r)$ such that $B((0,0), r) \subseteq R$. However, for any $r > 0$, the point $\left( -\frac{r}{2}, -\frac{r}{2} \right)$ belongs to $B((0,0), r)$ but does not belong to $R$. This is a contradiction. Thus, $R$ is not open in the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$. Hence, $\mathcal{T} $ is strictly finer than the usual topology on $\mathbb{R} ^2$.

problems.title: NBHM PhD 2005 - Topology of invertible matrices
problems.problem:
Identify the space of all $n \times n$ matrices (with real entries) with $\mathbb{R} ^n$. Let $A$ be the set of all invertible matrices. Which of the topological among the following does $A$ satisfy?
  1. Open
  2. Closed
  3. Connected
  4. Nowhere dense.
problems.hint: • Look at the map $\det : M(n,\mathbb{R} ) \to \mathbb{R} $.
problems.solution:
We will denote \begin{align*} M(n,\mathbb{R} ) & = \text{ Set of all } n \times n \text{ matrices with real entries} \\ GL(n,\mathbb{R} ) & = \text{ Set of all } n \times n \text{ invertible matrices with real entries}. \end{align*} We will show that $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is open, not closed, connected and not nowhere dense.
First, we show that $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is open. Note that a matrix $A$ is invertible if and only if $\det(A) \neq 0$. The determinant function $$\det: M(n,\mathbb{R} ) \to \mathbb{R}, \quad A \mapsto \det(A) $$ is continuous. Since $GL(n,\mathbb{R} ) = \det^{-1} (\mathbb{R} \setminus \{ 0 \})$, and $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{ 0 \}$ is open in $\mathbb{R} $, it follows that $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is open in $M(n,\mathbb{R} )$. \vspace{0.3cm} \noindent Next, we show that $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is not closed. Consider the sequence of matrices \[ A_k = \mathrm{diag} \left( \frac{1}{k}, \frac{1}{k}, \ldots, \frac{1}{k} \right) \in GL(n,\mathbb{R} ). \] The sequence $\{A_k\}$ converges to the zero matrix $O$, which is not invertible. Thus, $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ does not contain all its limit points and hence is not closed. \vspace{0.3cm} \noindent Now we show that $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is not connected. Consider the map \[ f: GL(n,\mathbb{R} ) \to \mathbb{R} \setminus \{ 0 \}, \quad A \mapsto \det(A). \] The map $f$ is continuous and surjective. Since $\mathbb{R} \setminus \{ 0 \}$ is disconnected (it has two components: the positive reals and the negative reals), the preimage of each component under $f$ is a nonempty open subset of $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$. \[ GL^+(n,\mathbb{R} ) = f^{-1} ((0, \infty)) \quad \text{and} \quad GL^-(n,\mathbb{R} ) = f^{-1} ((-\infty, 0)). \] Both $GL^+(n,\mathbb{R} )$ and $GL^-(n,\mathbb{R} )$ are nonempty, open, and disjoint subsets of $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ whose union is $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$. Thus, $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is not connected. \vspace{0.3cm} \noindent Finally, we show that $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is not nowhere dense in $M(n,\mathbb{R} )$. Recall that a nowhere dense set is a set whose closure has empty interior. Since $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is open, so for every $A \in GL(n, \mathbb{R} )$ there exists an open ball around $A$ contained in $GL(n, \mathbb{R} )$. This implies that $A \in \mathrm{int}(\mathrm{Cl}(GL(n, \mathbb{R} )))$. Thus, $\mathrm{int}(\mathrm{Cl}(GL(n, \mathbb{R} )))$ is not empty and hence $GL(n,\mathbb{R} )$ is not nowhere dense in $M(n,\mathbb{R} )$.
